pageProps.body:
Static Factor Model Inference

\newcommand{\sumT}{\sum{t = 1}^{T}} \newcommand{\sumTs}{\sum{s = 1}^{T}} \newcommand{\sumN}{\sum{i = 1}^{N}} \newcommand{\sumNj}{\sum{j = 1}^{N}} \newcommand{\convp}{\overset{p}{\to}} \newcommand{\convd}{\overset{d}{\to}} \newcommand{\limN}{\lim{N \to \infty}} \newcommand{\limT}{\lim{T \to \infty}} \newcommand{\plimT}{\operatorname{plim}{T \to \infty}} \newcommand{\plimN}{\operatorname{plim}{N \to \infty}} \newcommand{\plimNT}{\operatorname{plim}{N,T \to \infty}} \newcommand{\ceil}[1]{\left \lceil #1 \right \rceil } \newcommand{\floor}[1]{\left \lfloor #1 \right \rfloor } \newcommand{\sumTfloor}{\sum{t = 1}^{\floor{\tau T}}} \newcommand{\sumTfloort}{\sum_{t = \floor{\tau T + 1}}^{T}}

High Level Overview

See Barigozzi LSE Notes notes for a broader, high level interpretation of these. In summary, a set of valid regularity conditions requires that:

  1. The factors to be pervasive, i.e. have non-negligible effects on most of the variables
  2. The idiosyncratic components to be weakly cross-correlated

Bai and Ng Regularity Conditions

[@bai_determining_2002 (Private)] develop the following 4 assumptions required for the estimation of the number of static factors. These are considered to be fairly standard assumptions within the literature.

Assumptions 1-4 are sufficient for the consistent estimation for the factors, their loadings, (up to an arbitrary rotation) and the number of factors.

In what follows below, ∣∣A∣∣=[tr(A′A)]1/2||A|| = [tr(A'A)]^{1/2}∣∣A∣∣=[tr(A′A)]1/2 denotes the Frobenius norm.

  1. Factors (Assumption F)
E∣∣Ft0∣∣<∞1T\sumTFt0Ft0′\convpΣF(r×r)p.s.d.E||F_t^0|| < \infty \\ \frac{1}{T} \sumT F_t^0 F_t^{0 \prime} \convp \Sigma_F (r \times r) p.s.d.E∣∣Ft0​∣∣<∞T1​\sumTFt0​Ft0′​\convpΣF​(r×r)p.s.d.

This assumption allows FtF_tFt​ to be dynamic such that A(L)Ft=ϵtA(L)F_t = \epsilon_tA(L)Ft​=ϵt​, but does not allow the dynamics to enter into XitX_{it}Xit​ directly, and is therefore still a static relationship.

It is in essence a moment condition ensuring that each factor has non-trivial contribution to the variance of XitX_{it}Xit​. Note that the true factors Ft0F_t^0Ft0​ are assumed to be stationary.

  1. Factor Loadings
∣∣λi∣∣≤λˉ≤∞,∣∣Λ0′Λ0/N−ΣN∣∣\convp0,ΣN>0,(r×r)|| \lambda_i || \leq \bar{\lambda} \leq \infty, \\ || \Lambda^{0 \prime} \Lambda^0 / N - \Sigma_N|| \convp 0, \Sigma_N > 0, (r \times r)∣∣λi​∣∣≤λˉ≤∞,∣∣Λ0′Λ0/N−ΣN​∣∣\convp0,ΣN​>0,(r×r)

Similarly, this ensures that each factor has a non-trivial contribution to the variance of XitX_{it}Xit​. The loadings are allowed to be random in this simple setup, provided that they are independent from the factors and idiosyncratic errors, and E∣∣λi∣∣4≤ME||\lambda_i || ^4 \leq ME∣∣λi​∣∣4≤M.

  1. Time and cross-sectional dependence and hetero-skedasticity

a. E(eit)=0,E∣eit∣8<ME(e_{it}) = 0, E|e_{it}|^8 < ME(eit​)=0,E∣eit​∣8<M b. E(eitejs)=σij,ts,∣σij,ts∣≤σijˉ∀(t,s),∣σij,ts∣≤τts∀(i,j)E(e_{it} e_{js}) = \sigma_{ij, ts}, \\ |\sigma_{ij, ts}| \leq \bar{\sigma_{ij}} \forall (t, s), \\ |\sigma_{ij, ts}| \leq \tau_{ts} \forall (i, j)E(eit​ejs​)=σij,ts​,∣σij,ts​∣≤σij​ˉ​∀(t,s),∣σij,ts​∣≤τts​∀(i,j), such that:

  • 1/N∑i,j=1Nσijˉ≤M1/N \sum_{i, j = 1}^N \bar{\sigma_{ij}} \leq M1/N∑i,j=1N​σij​ˉ​≤M,
  • 1/T∑t,s=1Nτts≤M1/T \sum_{t, s = 1}^N \tau_{ts} \leq M1/T∑t,s=1N​τts​≤M,
  • 1/NT∑i,j,t,s=1∣σij,ts∣≤M1/NT \sum_{i, j, t, s = 1} |\sigma_{ij, ts}| \leq M1/NT∑i,j,t,s=1​∣σij,ts​∣≤M
  1. Weak correlation between Factors and Errors

E(1/N\sumN∣∣T−1/2\sumTFteit)∣∣2≤ME(1/N \sumN ||T^{-1/2} \sumT F_t e_{it}) ||^2 \leq ME(1/N\sumN∣∣T−1/2\sumTFt​eit​)∣∣2≤M

Under these 4 assumptions, [@bai_determining_2002 (Private)] show that the PCA estimator F^t\widehat{F}_tFt​ is able to consistently estimate the true factors up to an arbitrary rotation:

1T\sumT∣∣F^t−H′Ft∣∣=OP(δNT−2)\frac{1}{T} \sumT ||\widehat{F}_t - H'F_t|| = O_P(\delta_{NT}^{-2})T1​\sumT∣∣Ft​−H′Ft​∣∣=OP​(δNT−2​)

where δNT=min⁡(T,N)\delta_{NT} = \operatorname{min}({\sqrt{T}, \sqrt{N}})δNT​=min(T​,N​), H=(Λ′Λ/N)(F′F^/T)VNT−1H = (\Lambda' \Lambda/N)(F' \widehat{F}/T)V_{NT}^{-1}H=(Λ′Λ/N)(F′F/T)VNT−1​, and VNTV_{NT}VNT​ is a diagonal matrix consisting of the first r eigenvalues of $XX'/NT in descending order.

[@bai_inferential_2003 (Private)] then further develop/supplement the previous 4 assumptions with the following, in order to develop the asymptotic behaviour of the PCA estimator for the factors and their loadings. Note that because the previous 4 assumptions can already achieve consistency of the PCA estimator (which is what most people care about anyway), the following 4 assumptions are not as well used.

  1. Strengthening of Assumption 3 earlier.

For all t≤Tt \leq Tt≤T, i≤Ni \leq Ni≤N, \sumTs∣τs,t∣≤M\sumTs |\tau_{s, t}| \leq M\sumTs∣τs,t​∣≤M, and \sumN∣σijˉ∣≤M\sumN |\bar{\sigma_{ij}}| \leq M\sumN∣σij​ˉ​∣≤M

Note that under time series and cross sectional independence, both parts of this assumption are already implied.

  1. Moments and Central Limit Theorem

  • E∣1/N\sumN[eiseit−E(eiseit)]∣4≤ME|1/\sqrt{N} \sumN [e_{is} e_{it} - E(e_{is} e_{it})] |^4 \leq ME∣1/N​\sumN[eis​eit​−E(eis​eit​)]∣4≤M for all (t,s)(t, s)(t,s)

  • The r×rr \times rr×r matrix satisfies

    E∣∣1NT\sumT\sumNFtλk′ekt∣∣≤ME|| \frac{1}{NT} \sumT \sumN F_t \lambda_k' e_{kt}|| \leq ME∣∣NT1​\sumT\sumNFt​λk′​ekt​∣∣≤M

  • For each ttt, 1/N\sumNλieit\convdN(0,Γt)1/\sqrt{N} \sumN \lambda_i e_{it} \convd N(0, \Gamma_t)1/N​\sumNλi​eit​\convdN(0,Γt​) as N→∞N \to \inftyN→∞, where

    Γt=\limN1N\sumN\sumNjE(λiλj′eitejt).\Gamma_t = \limN \frac{1}{N} \sumN \sumNj E(\lambda_i \lambda_j' e_{it} e_{jt}).Γt​=\limNN1​\sumN\sumNjE(λi​λj′​eit​ejt​).

  • For each iii, 1/T\sumTFteit\convdN(0,Φi)1/\sqrt{T} \sumT F_t e_{it} \convd N(0, \Phi_i)1/T​\sumTFt​eit​\convdN(0,Φi​) as T→∞T \to \inftyT→∞, where:

    Φi=\limT1/T\sumTs\sumTE(Ft0Fs0′eiseit)\Phi_i = \limT 1/T \sumTs \sumT E(F_t^0 F_s^{0 \prime} e_{is} e_{it})Φi​=\limT1/T\sumTs\sumTE(Ft0​Fs0′​eis​eit​)

This assumption allows the number of strongly correlated errors to grow at a rate slower than N\sqrt{N}N​. Note that the last two parts are basically CLTs.

  1. The eigenvalues of ΣΛ,ΣF\Sigma_\Lambda, \Sigma_FΣΛ​,ΣF​ are distinct.

These make up the total 7 assumptions/regularity conditions, typically referred to as the Bai and Ng regularity conditions.

PCA Asymptotics

Let:

  • V=\plimNTVNTV = \plimNT V_{NT}V=\plimNTVNT​
  • Q=\plimNTF^t′FTQ = \plimNT \frac{\widehat{F}_t' F}{T}Q=\plimNTTFt′​F​

Under the previous 7 assumptions, [@bai_critical_2003 (Private)] shows that:

  1. Asymptotic Normality of Factors. If N/T→0\sqrt{N}/T \to 0N​/T→0, then for each ttt

    N(Ft^−H′Ft0)=VNT−1(F^′FT)1N\sumNλieit+op(1)\convdN(0,V−1QΓtQ′V−1)\sqrt{N}(\widehat{F_t} - H' F_t^0) = V_{NT}^{-1} \left( \frac{\widehat{F}' F}{T} \right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sumN \lambda_i e_{it} + o_p(1) \\ \convd N(0, V^{-1} Q \Gamma_t Q' V^{-1})N​(Ft​​−H′Ft0​)=VNT−1​(TF′F​)N​1​\sumNλi​eit​+op​(1)\convdN(0,V−1QΓt​Q′V−1)

  2. Asymptotic Normality of loadings. If T/N→0\sqrt{T}/N \to 0T​/N→0, then for each iii

    T(λi^−H−1λi)=(F^t′FT)(Λ′ΛN)1T\sumTFteit+op(1)\convdN(0,Q−′ΦiQ−1)\sqrt{T}(\widehat{\lambda_i} - H^{-1}\lambda_i) = \left( \frac{\widehat{F}_t' F}{T} \right) \left( \frac{\Lambda' \Lambda}{N} \right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}} \sumT F_t e_{it} + o_p(1) \\ \convd N(0, Q^{-\prime} \Phi_i Q^{-1})T​(λi​​−H−1λi​)=(TFt′​F​)(NΛ′Λ​)T​1​\sumTFt​eit​+op​(1)\convdN(0,Q−′Φi​Q−1)

  3. Asymptotic Normality of Common Component.

Note that because NNN and TTT are likely to be similar to in size to one another in practice, both of the above results are likely to hold, and the above distributions are regarded as good approximations in good samples.

PCA Identification

Confusingly, due to the multiplicative structure of FΛ′F \Lambda'FΛ′, there is a notable identification issue: FA(ΛA−1)′FA (\Lambda A^{-1})'FA(ΛA−1)′ are observationally equivalent for any arbitrary non-singular AAA matrix. Because of this, at least r2r^2r2 restrictions need to be imposed to separately identify the FFF and Λ\LambdaΛ.

PCA implicitly imposes the following identification schemes:

  1. F^t′F^/T=Ir\widehat{F}_t' \widehat{F}/T = I_rFt′​F/T=Ir​, and Λ^′Λ^\widehat{\Lambda}' \widehat{\Lambda}Λ′Λ is diagonal. This corresponds to the "tilde" normalisation. The estimated factors are set to T\sqrt{T}T​ times the eigenvectors corresponding to the eigenvalues of XX′,T×TXX', T \times TXX′,T×T

  2. Λ^t′Λ^/N=Ir\widehat{\Lambda}_t' \widehat{\Lambda}/N = I_rΛt′​Λ/N=Ir​, and \F^′\F^\widehat{\F}' \widehat{\F}\F​′\F​ is diagonal. This corresponds to the "bar" normalisation. The estimated factors are set to N\sqrt{N}N​ times the eigenvectors corresponding to the eigenvalues of X′X,N×NX'X, N \times NX′X,N×N.

In order to deal with the possible structural breaks, several authors bring in:

  1. Identification condition for structural changes in factor loadings
T−1\sumTfloorFtFt′\convpτΣFT−1\sumTfloortFtFt′\convp(1−τ)ΣFT^{-1} \sumTfloor F_t F_t' \convp \tau \Sigma_F \\ T^{-1} \sumTfloort F_t F_t' \convp (1 - \tau) \Sigma_FT−1\sumTfloorFt​Ft′​\convpτΣF​T−1\sumTfloortFt​Ft′​\convp(1−τ)ΣF​

Note that we have used τ\tauτ instead of π\piπ, as some other authors do.

Bai and Ng 2006

[@bai_confidence_2006 (Private)] derive additional convergence rates for the PC estimator. Notably, due to the convergence rates derived, they show that it is OK to treat the estimated factors as subsequent regressors.

This is mostly relevant for formally justifying the use of estimated factors in a subsequent regression, say a FAVAR model.

Barigozzi Assumptions

[@barigozzi_simultaneous_2018 (Private)] instead follows the strand of literature in the generalized dynamic factor models (truly dynamic) and uses an alternative set of assumptions.

For the most part, these are very similar.

For practical purposes, it does not matter which set - simply choose whichever one is easier for the problem at hand.

Children

  1. r Estimators

noteIndex.title: PhD Homepage
noteIndex.body:
Welcome to my website!

This website has been built using Dendron, and each topic has been collated into collapsible sections on the left hand side. Below is supposed to be a graph view so that you can visualize and explore how topics are connected to one another, but this has not been implemented yet.

The search functionality is very powerful. Note that default search tries to exact match actual page names - make sure to prefix with ? to get actual search.

## Latest Meeting Agenda

Main points:

- 


Ideas for new test statistic:

Question:

How many *static* factors are there subject to breaks?

- This is mostly answered, IC are consistent for an equivalent *static* representation without breaks (proposition in [@han_tests_2015], and a comment in [@baltagi_estimating_2021])
    - These are not that easy to work with, but this covers almost all cases of breaks
    - A direct implication of this is that structural breaks will *always increase* the no. of static factors estimated
- However, representation theorems have not considered any breaks in factors that cannot be explained by a change in loadings, due to the multiplicative structure
    - change in variance of primitive shocks (heteroskedasticity)
    - mean shift in factor process

Does overestimation/underestimation actually affect subsequent forecasting?
- This is a good opportunity to tie in with
    - Machine Learning thesis (this was essentially forecasting), demonstrating one way which ML methods can break down
    - CWF project, which ideally could offer a possible solution to this (or at least provide a method that is more robust to breaks)

- Test for structural change one time, vs smooth changes (non parametrics), kink vs curve
- Test for change in variance of primitive shock innovations (this does not seem to be covered yet)
- Test for changes in factor dynamics, based on the assumption that the number of primitive factors does not change (seems reasonable enough in economics...?)

Housekeeping

- macroXTS class is finished
- static $r$ IC have been implemented
- standard PCA estimator should be fully implemented
- dynamic $q$ IC are all WIP
- other estimators of DFMs are not yet implemented
    - EM estimator (viewed as QMLE)
    - Bayesian (can of worms)
    - Spectral density estimator (useful for getting the actual primitive shocks)
